justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (Prediction Service with in-memory inference) pipelines are thoroughly designed. 100ms P99 constraint is central to the design — addressed via embedded inference (<1ms), Redis feature fetch (<10ms), and explicit latency budget breakdown. Feature store identified as essential with dual online/offline paths. Model versioning with rollback via Model Registry pointer swap and blue/green loading. Team size factored throughout — recommends managed services, embedded inference over Triton, manual promotion over automated canary, explicit 2+2 team split. Trade-offs and risks are extensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed latency budget breakdown (10ms features + 1ms inference + 5ms overhead) demonstrates deep understanding of the 100ms constraint
Training-serving skew identified as critical risk with concrete mitigation (shared feature library)
Phased implementation plan starting with rule-based baseline is pragmatic for a 4-person team
Blue/green model loading pattern enables zero-downtime rollback
Feature staleness and model drift risks explicitly called out with mitigations
Recommends LightGBM/XGBoost over deep learning with clear rationale tied to latency and team constraints

trials.justification: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (Prediction API with in-process inference) pipelines are designed in detail. The 100ms P99 constraint is thoroughly addressed — it's treated as a hard constraint driving the in-process inference pattern, model complexity guidance (gradient-boosted trees, not deep learning), and the explicit rejection of external service hops. Model versioning and rollback are well-designed via Model Registry with active pointer, blue-green deployment, and a dedicated Model Deployment Controller. Team size is factored throughout — recommending simple S3+Postgres over MLflow, cron over Airflow, no Kubernetes — which is an equally valid (arguably better) approach to 'managed services' for a 4-person team. Trade-offs and risks are extensive and high-quality. The one rubric tension: MJ explicitly rejects a feature store, arguing inline computation of 6 features is faster and simpler. This is a defensible architectural call with clear reasoning — the underlying concern (pre-computed features, no real-time feature computation adding latency) is addressed via shared feature encoding and the assumption that inputs arrive pre-computed from the checkout service. The spirit of the requirement is met even though the specific component is rejected.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed serving path design with in-process inference, SIGHUP-based model reload, and rolling restarts — goes well beyond the minimum
Shadow evaluation service is a sophisticated addition that validates models on real traffic before promotion — more robust than simple A/B testing for a small team
Feature skew prevention (shared feature encoding library) identified as the single most important pattern — shows deep ML engineering understanding
Prediction logging with async fire-and-forget closes the feedback loop for retraining without impacting serving latency
Risk analysis is production-grade: cold start latency, logging backpressure, team bottleneck, feature staleness all identified with concrete mitigations
Model complexity constrained by latency requirement — gradient-boosted trees recommended with clear reasoning against neural networks
Explicitly rejects feature store — while well-reasoned for 6 features, this contradicts the rubric expectation. If feature count grows, the architecture would need retrofitting

Does not recommend managed ML services (SageMaker, Vertex AI) — instead recommends custom lightweight infrastructure. Valid for simplicity but the rubric expected managed services for team size consideration
Implementation phasing (6 weeks, clear ownership split between ML and platform engineers) is practical and actionable for the team size

trials.justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (real-time prediction service) pipelines are fully designed with clear components and interfaces. The 100ms P99 constraint is thoroughly addressed with a latency budget breakdown (1ms feature fetch, 1ms inference, 3ms overhead, 95ms margin), in-process model serving to eliminate network hops, and pre-computed features. Feature Store is identified as necessary with Redis for online and S3/Parquet for offline, explicitly preventing train-serve skew. Model versioning is comprehensive with a Model Registry (S3 + PostgreSQL), blue-green deployment, champion/challenger evaluation gate, and automatic rollback on degradation. Team size is factored throughout — recommending in-process serving over TFServing/Triton, LightGBM over deep learning, and explicitly warning against infrastructure a 4-person team cannot sustain. Trade-offs and risks are extensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed architecture with 7 well-defined components, clear boundaries, and explicit interface contracts
Latency budget breakdown (1ms + 1ms + 3ms + 95ms margin) demonstrates deep understanding of the P99 constraint
Train-serve skew identified as 'the #1 silent killer of ML systems' with Feature Store as the rigid mitigation
Blue-green model deployment with automatic rollback on MAE degradation exceeds the rollback requirement
Team size consideration is woven throughout, not just a footnote — influences model choice, serving architecture, and infrastructure decisions
Graceful degradation with feature defaults fallback shows production-readiness thinking
Phased implementation guidance with concrete technology choices (FastAPI, ONNX Runtime, LightGBM, Redis) is actionable
Flexibility and rigidity points clearly distinguish what can evolve from what must remain fixed
